title: Transform Your Stable Diffusion: Theme & Color Customization Tutorial!
description:
🌟 Transform Your Stable Diffusion Interface - Full Customization Tutorial 

In this tutorial, we dive deep into customizing your Stable Diffusion interface, moving beyond the basic look to something that truly reflects your style. Whether it’s adopting a sleek dark theme or adding a personal touch with your brand colors, this guide covers it all. Perfect for users of Stable Diffusion Automatic 1111 on Windows, we'll walk you through simple yet impactful modifications.

What You'll Learn:

1. Activating Dark Mode: Learn how to switch to dark mode with a simple URL tweak and make it your default setting.
2. Exploring Themes in Gradio: Discover a variety of themes including the 'Glass' theme, and learn how to apply them.
3. Customizing Button Colors: A step-by-step guide to changing button colors to your preferred shades, including how to edit the colors.py file.
4. Adjusting Sliders and More: Detailed instructions on modifying slider colors and other interface elements for a cohesive loo
transcript: Are you bored with your basic stable diffusion interface?  Spice it up.  Try new colors and themes to make it uniquely yours.  Start customizing today and see the difference.  I am using stable diffusion automatic 1111 installed  on Windows, and the easiest way to quickly change the interface  is to add a slash to your IP address in the address bar.  A question mark followed by two underscores,  the word theme, an equal sign, dark, and then press  enter.  You should then see your interface in dark mode.  However, this change will only last for this session.  If you want to start in this mode each time,  go to your WebUI folder and edit the WebUI user .bat file,  which can be opened with notepad or notepad++ a free program.  In the arguments line, if you don't have anything there,  just add it after the equal sign.  Or if you have something, leave a space and add it  at the end of the line with line, line, theme, space, dark.  Save it, then restart your stable diffusion  by closing your command prompt window  and running the bat file again.  As you can see, it now starts with the argument  for the dark theme, setting it as the default.  So even if you delete the text from the address bar,  it will still return to this to remove it,  just delete it from the bat file.  And go to the settings tab and scroll down  to find user interface.  There, you'll see Gradio theme set to default,  but a list of other themes is available for you to choose from.  For example, let's test the Glass theme.  Click Apply Settings, then reload UI.  The interface will reload in a few seconds with the new theme.  Return to Settings and the user interface.  It's not easy to choose from this list  without seeing the themes first,  but you can preview these and other themes  by using Control plus click on the word gallery.  This action opens a new window with a gallery of themes.  Here, you can opt to show only the dark mode themes  and check all the themes available,  ensuring they work without errors.  For instance, if you want to preview a theme  called Small and Pretty,  click on it to see a preview,  click toggle dark to view it in dark mode.  To install this theme, note the text,  between the quotes in the section to use this theme.  In this case, John Smith, Small and Pretty,  go back to your Gradio theme selection  where all the theme names are listed and paste that text there.  Finally, click on Apply Settings and then on Reload UI.  It looks interesting, but let me show you how I change my theme  to the colors I want.  First, I went back to Settings and set it to the default theme.  I like this theme color,  but I wanted a different button color,  specifically my brand colors instead of orange.  To do this, I had to find the files responsible  for those colors.  In the Web UI folder, go to the VenV folder,  then lib, site packages, and look for the Gradio folder.  Next, navigate to themes and finally, to utils.  Here, you'll find a file called colors.pi,  always make a copy before editing something,  so you can return to the original if you mess up.  Then, open this with notepad.  As you can see, some colors like gray and slate  are declared here with various names.  Now, I created a new color name, Pixoroma,  using the same style and format I observed here,  where it says Pixoroma.  You can use any color name that doesn't exist  in the document like pink or whatever.  Here, shading on different colors is present.  I asked ChatGPT to give me all those color variations  based on my desired color.  If you search on Google for a color picker,  you can use it to pick color you like.  So, I want this green color to appear on the interface.  You can also edit an existing color from there if you want,  but for me, it seemed easier just to add my color there.  Save the document,  and let's check another document where we use the color.  We just saved.  Look for the default.pi file, make a copy of it,  and open the file in notepad.  Now, look for primary and secondary hues,  which should be set to orange and blue by default.  Replace these with your color.  In my case, it's the Pixoroma color I declared earlier.  Save the document and test to see how it works  by restarting stable diffusion.  I now have the green button in the color I wanted,  as well as the check marks in the same color.  However, if you look at the sliders, they are blue,  and I want them green.  So, it was time to dig more into the files  until I found the slider.  Eventually, I found a file named base.pi.  Make a copy of it and open it.  If you scroll down, you can see some comments  explaining what each setting does.  I searched for slider and found two options.  Since I will use the dark theme,  I replace the none from the dark slider with my color code,  making sure it is inside quotation marks.  Save it and restart stable diffusion.  Everything seems to look how I want it,  and probably there are more things that can be changed,  but for now, it does the job until I get bored again  and start digging.  For more settings, I can change.  Here's another useful tip.  Let's say you have some settings you like  and want them as defaults when you open stable diffusion,  like image size, a prompt, or CFG scale.  You can change all the settings to your liking.  For example, I use some negative prompts,  so I will add that.  Go to settings, search for defaults,  and click the View Changes button  to see all the changes I made like the prompt,  some sampling steps, and the image size.  Click the Apply button, then reload the UI.  Now, after a restart, all the settings are there by default.  All those settings are saved in the UIconfig JSON file.  Make a copy of that before you save anything,  just in case something doesn't work.  You can restore that file and it's like brand new.  If you look inside your file,  you can find all kinds of settings,  including the ones I saved,  like the negative prompt and many more.
